summary: Gruber examines a 2011 email from Phil Schiller to Steve Jobs proposing that Apple proactively reduce its App Store commission from 70/30, arguing it would have prevented many of Apple's current antitrust and developer relations problems. He contends that Apple's insistence on maximizing App Store revenue — a relative side hustle compared to hardware — has damaged developer goodwill and invited regulatory scrutiny that was entirely avoidable. Gruber emphasizes that the developers most critical of Apple are precisely those most committed to its platforms, and their dissatisfaction is primarily about money. He argues that developer goodwill, like the Apple logo, is priceless, and Schiller's decade-old memo came close to putting a price tag on it.
key_points: Phil Schiller's 2011 email to Jobs proposed proactively reducing the App Store commission before competitive pressure forced Apple's hand, a plan Apple never followed
Apple's antitrust problems worldwide are almost entirely centered on the App Store, and many would disappear if the revenue split had been reduced years ago
The developers most critical of App Store policies are those most deeply committed to Apple's platforms — the ones Apple should most want to keep happy
The App Store is a side hustle relative to Apple's overall business, making it uniquely bizarre that it has become the company's primary antitrust liability
Apple's leadership genuinely believes their App Store policies are fair, but that righteousness doesn't change the reality of widespread developer dissatisfaction
money_quotes: This email is simultaneously not surprising — because he's Phil Schiller, steward of the Apple brand — but also shocking, because, my god, it spells out a game plan that would have kept Apple out of all this.
The App Store, though, feels more and more like the one area of the company where they've committed to squeezing as much money as they can out of it.
Most developers I know think that the only thing Apple turns upside down for developers is the proverbial couch, out of which Apple seemingly wants to shake every last nickel of spare change it can.
What's weirdest about Apple's antitrust and PR problems related to the App Store is that the App Store is a side hustle for Apple.
To my knowledge no company in history has ever gotten into antitrust hot water over a side business so comparatively small to its overall business. Apple doesn't need this.

Like the Apple logo, 'developer goodwill' has no price tag. But Phil Schiller's decade-ago idea to start dialing down the revenue split — in favor of developers — comes pretty close to assigning it one.
I don't think the developers are wrong, but even if they are wrong, it's not good for Apple that they're so unhappy, and feel so aggrieved.
key_insight: Apple's refusal to proactively reduce App Store commissions — despite internal recognition a decade ago that the 70/30 split wouldn't last forever — has cost the company developer goodwill and invited antitrust scrutiny over what amounts to a side business.
